top_categories.workout_context: 8,990ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Eroica Dolomiti's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before September.
68 miles on mixed terrain burns energy faster than the distance suggests. Train the fade resistance now.
132ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Eroica Dolomiti's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 68 miles.
Base fitness for Eroica Dolomiti. Without this, the hard workouts break you down instead of building you up.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Eroica Dolomiti. Train it until it feels boring.
At Eroica Dolomiti, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 8,990ft of climbing on mixed terrain does to your power file on race day.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on mixed terrain. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
Double-threshold training builds sustained power with less fatigue. More FTP per hour of training.
Below LT1, you burn fat. Above it, you burn glycogen. For 68 miles, a higher LT1 means slower bonk.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 40–60g carbs/hour for Eroica Dolomiti’s 68 miles. Front-load calories in the first half. One bottle per hour minimum, more in heat.
race_overlay.altitude: Eroica Dolomiti with 8,990ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on VO2max, Race Simulation, and Durability to prepare you for 68 miles of mixed terrain in San Candido, South Tyrol, Italy.
